metric_value: Tivoli had competitive disadvantage because it could not deliver production-grade security services that competitors already provided
notes: Aberdeen direct quote: 'Until now, Tivoli has been at a competitive disadvantage'

metric_name: Portfolio gap filled by TME 10 SM
metric_value: TME 10 SM fills a glaring void in Tivoli's product portfolio by adding enterprise-class security management
notes: Aberdeen assessment of strategic significance

metric_value: Supports HP-UX/RISC; IBM OS/390-RACF and AIX/RISC; Windows NT Server/Intel; Novell NetWare; SunOS and Solaris/RISC — via TME v3.1+
notes: Five major platform families covered in initial release

metric_value: Role-based access control (RBAC) using system-independent security profile-records distributed over network; non-intrusive installation without taking servers down
notes: Key architectural differentiator: business-process-oriented rather than technology-focused security

metric_value: Solutions too complicated and brittle (DCE/Kerberos/Sesame) requiring expensive integration
notes: Aberdeen field research: IS executives identify this as primary barrier

metric_value: Solutions non-interoperable placing additional burden on IS budget and deployment schedules
notes: Second major IS complaint: cross-platform interoperability failure

metric_value: Solutions technology-focused instead of organizational and business-process focused
notes: Third pain point: security tools not aligned to business policy

metric_value: Most solutions limited to protecting one operating environment (NetWare-only / Unix-only / NT-only / OS/390-only) requiring separate admin per platform
notes: Drives the core value proposition of TME 10 SM

metric_value: Passwords (default); DCE and Kerberos for admin roles; time-of-day/day-of-week login restrictions; location-based login restrictions; password quality enforcement
notes: Authentication feature set documented

metric_value: Four-element RBAC: system-wide policies; group authorizations; role authorizations; IT resources — all managed centrally via security profile-records
notes: Foundation of TME 10 SM's business-process security model

metric_value: Security profile-records pushed to subscribing nodes enabling local operation with central management; TACF for Unix partitions root account privileges to prevent compromise
notes: Key technical differentiator: push-based profile distribution

metric_value: Partner Exchange (TPE) program with 12+ security ISV partners to integrate point solutions: Axent; Cybersafe; Checkpoint Software; Cygnus; Dynasoft; Haystack Labs; IBM; ICL; ISS; Mergent; Memco; Trusted Information Systems
notes: Strategy to position TME 10 SM as integration framework rather than point solution

metric_value: Plans to add ACF2/TOP SECRET on mainframe; Oracle/Informix/Sybase databases; OS/2; DCE; web servers; smart card/public-key authentication; SSO; secure commerce
notes: Roadmap items from Tivoli announced directions

metric_value: TME 10 SM will serve as cornerstone for integrating security components; IS decision makers can depend on it for managing security through IT transitions
notes: Aberdeen forward-looking endorsement

metric_value: IBM evolved TME 10 into IBM Tivoli Identity Manager (2001) and later IBM Security Identity Manager; Tivoli brand phased out 2013; technology survived as IBM Security Verify
notes: IBM Security Verify (formerly IBM Security Identity Governance and Intelligence) still active 2024

metric_value: Tivoli Systems acquired by IBM for $743M in 1996 before this study was published; study written under IBM ownership
notes: IBM paid $47.50/share; Tivoli had ~$50M annual revenue at time of acquisition

metric_name: RACF as gold standard for security
metric_value: RACF described as proven mainframe security; enterprise desire is RACF-equivalent for distributed computing
notes: RACF used as benchmark reference throughout document

metric_value: NetWare security limited to NetWare-only platforms; incompatible with other enterprise systems
notes: Platform-specific security cited as major IS frustration

metric_value: Aberdeen recommends IS executives 'run — not walk — to evaluate the TME 10 Security Management solution'
notes: Strongest possible positive recommendation language

metric_value: DCE and Kerberos described as complicated and brittle security protocols requiring expensive lengthy in-house integration
notes: IS practitioners characterize DCE/Kerberos as too complex for widespread deployment
